title: Message Queue — System Design
description: Designing a distributed message queue / log (think Kafka or SQS): producers append messages, consumers read them, and the system guarantees durability, ordering within a partition, and well-defined delivery semantics at high throughput. The interesting problems are the classic distributed-systems ones: partitioning for scale, offsets and consumer groups, at-least-once vs exactly-once effect, ordering, acknowledgement/visibility, replication, and dead-lettering. We build from an in-memory queue toward a partitioned, replicated, consumer-group log.

nodes.label: Ack / Visibility Tracker
nodes.description: Ack / Visibility Tracker: Owns per-message lease state in work-queue mode — visibility timeouts, receipt handles, retry counts, and DLQ routing.

nodes.label: Assignment Store
nodes.description: Assignment Store: Shared store consumers use to claim partition ownership in client-side cooperative assignment (no central coordinator).

nodes.label: Consumer Dedup Store
nodes.description: Consumer Dedup Store: Per-consumer store of seen business keys used to make at-least-once processing idempotent.

nodes.label: Broker
nodes.description: Broker: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Consumer 1
nodes.description: Consumer 1: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Consumer 2
nodes.description: Consumer 2: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Consumer
nodes.description: Consumer: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Group Coordinator
nodes.description: Group Coordinator: Workflow component that coordinates multi-step operations or compensation.

nodes.label: Dead-Letter Queue
nodes.description: Dead-Letter Queue: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Follower Replica
nodes.description: Follower Replica: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Consumer Group
nodes.description: Consumer Group: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Dedup Store
nodes.description: Dedup Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Leader Replica
nodes.description: Leader Replica: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Message Table
nodes.description: Message Table: A relational/mutable row-per-message table with a consumed flag (the rejected alternative to an append-only log).

nodes.label: Controller / Metadata Quorum
nodes.description: Controller / Metadata Quorum: Control-plane quorum that owns partition metadata — leader assignment, ISR membership, leader/controller epochs — and fences stale leaders and producers.

nodes.label: Consumer Offsets
nodes.description: Consumer Offsets: Authoritative or operational state store for records, metadata, counters, locks, or durable state.

nodes.label: Partition 0
nodes.description: Partition 0: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Partition 1
nodes.description: Partition 1: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Partition 2
nodes.description: Partition 2: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Producer
nodes.description: Producer: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Partition Router
nodes.description: Partition Router: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

functional: Producers publish messages to a named topic.
Consumers subscribe and read messages, tracking their position.
Messages are durable once acknowledged to the producer.
Support multiple independent consumer groups over the same topic.

Support two consumption contracts: a stream/log mode (consumers commit a group offset) and a work-queue mode (per-message lease, ack/delete, retry count, DLQ). This queue-vs-log choice is the first thing to pin down — it drives the API, data model, and failure modes.
Dead-letter messages that repeatedly fail processing (work-queue mode), or route them to an error topic (stream mode).
nonFunctional: High throughput: millions of messages per second.
Durability: an acknowledged message is not lost on broker failure.
Ordering within a partition is preserved.
Well-defined delivery semantics (at-least-once by default; exactly-once effect via dedup).
Horizontal scalability by adding partitions and brokers.
Consumers can fail and resume without losing or massively reprocessing messages.

capacity.label: Throughput
capacity.note: Sequential append to a log is the key to high write throughput.

capacity.label: Message size
capacity.note: Many small messages; batching amortizes per-message overhead.

capacity.label: Retention
capacity.note: The log retains messages for a window so consumers can replay.

capacity.label: Consumers
capacity.note: Each group reads independently at its own offset.

capacity.label: Durability
capacity.note: Acked messages survive broker failure via replication.

capacity.label: Ordering scope
capacity.note: Global ordering doesn't scale; ordering is guaranteed within a partition only.

capacity.label: Raw ingress
capacity.note: 1M msgs/s × 1 KB ≈ 1 GB/s write before replication. Batching + compression (say 3–5×) cuts on-wire/on-disk bytes.

capacity.label: Replicated write load
capacity.note: With replication factor 3 the cluster physically writes ~3× ingress before compression — the real disk/network budget.

capacity.label: Retention storage
capacity.note: 1 GB/s × 86,400 s ≈ 86 TB/day raw; ×3 for replicas ≈ 259 TB/day before compression and segment/index overhead.

capacity.label: Read fan-out
capacity.note: Each consumer group is an independent read stream; N caught-up groups add ~N× ingress in reads (mostly served from page cache when consumers keep up).

capacity.label: Partition / broker count
capacity.note: If one partition sustains ~10 MB/s, 1 GB/s needs ~100+ partitions; broker count follows from per-broker disk throughput and NIC (e.g. ~10–25 Gbps) plus headroom for replication and reads.

api.description: Publish a message. Optional key decides the partition; producer may batch. The broker dedups retries by (producer_id, producer_epoch, seq); producer_epoch fences a stale/zombie producer after a restart.

Stream/log mode. Poll for messages from the partitions assigned to this consumer group member, starting at the committed offset. The member identity (member_id, generation) lets the coordinator fence stale members after a rebalance.

Stream/log mode. Commit the consumer group's offset after processing. The coordinator rejects commits whose generation is older than the current one, so a consumer that lost ownership in a rebalance can't overwrite progress.
Work-queue mode. Receive up to N messages, each leased with a receipt handle for a visibility timeout; the message is invisible to other consumers until acked or the lease expires.

Work-queue mode. Acknowledge (delete) a leased message by its receipt handle. A stale handle (lease already expired and message redelivered) is rejected so a slow consumer can't delete someone else's in-flight copy.
api.response: 200 OK  (410 LEASE_EXPIRED if the lease has timed out)

dataModel.name: topics
dataModel.note: A named stream, split into partitions.

dataModel.name: partition_log
dataModel.note: Append-only, ordered sequence of records per (topic, partition). Offset is the position. Physically stored as segment files with sparse offset/time indexes; retention/compaction reclaim old segments.

dataModel.name: consumer_offsets
dataModel.note: Stream/log mode. Per (topic, group, partition) committed position, fenced by generation. The only consumer-side state.

dataModel.name: dedup_ids
dataModel.note: Producer idempotency state to dedupe retried publishes (exactly-once append within a bounded window). Keyed by producer; producer_epoch fences zombie producers.

dataModel.name: partition_metadata
dataModel.note: Control-plane metadata owned by the controller/quorum: who leads each partition, its replica set, in-sync set, and the epochs used to fence stale leaders.

dataModel.name: inflight (work-queue mode)
dataModel.note: Per-message lease state for work-queue consumption: who holds it, until when, and how many times it has been delivered. Absent in pure log mode.

patterns.name: Append-only log
patterns.what: Store messages as an immutable, ordered, append-only sequence; position = offset.
patterns.whenToUse: High-throughput durable streams where sequential writes and replay matter.

patterns.name: Partitioning by key
patterns.what: Split a topic into partitions; key hashing keeps a key's messages ordered on one partition.
patterns.whenToUse: Scaling throughput while preserving per-key ordering.

patterns.name: Consumer groups + offsets
patterns.what: Independent groups track their own offset; partitions are balanced across group members.
patterns.whenToUse: Multiple independent readers and horizontal consumer scaling.

patterns.name: At-least-once + idempotency
patterns.what: Redeliver until acked, and dedupe so the effect is exactly-once.

patterns.name: Visibility timeout / ack
patterns.what: Hide an in-flight message until acked or a timeout requeues it.
patterns.whenToUse: Queue-style consumption where a consumer may crash mid-process.

patterns.name: Dead-letter queue
patterns.what: Route repeatedly-failing messages aside instead of blocking or dropping.
patterns.whenToUse: Any retrying consumer with poison messages.

patterns.name: Leader-follower replication
patterns.what: Replicate each partition to followers; ack after replication for durability.
patterns.whenToUse: No-loss durability and availability under broker failure.

steps.title: 1. In-Memory Queue (the baseline)
steps.description: Start with a single broker holding an in-memory FIFO queue: producers push, one consumer pops. This is genuinely fine for local, ephemeral, best-effort work where losing a few in-flight items is acceptable. It fails our durability requirement, though: it works until the broker restarts (messages lost) or load exceeds one node — which is exactly why the rest of the design exists.
steps.decisionPrompt: What happens to un-consumed messages when the broker process restarts?

concepts.definition: The writer and reader of the queue.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decoupling them in time is the whole point of a queue.

talkingPoints: Name durability and single-node throughput as the two things this can't do.
Frame the log as the fix for both.

traps.why: A restart loses every un-consumed message.
traps.instead: Persist to a durable, append-only log.

bottlenecks.issue: One node caps throughput and loses data on crash.

strong: Immediately flags durability and single-node limits.
weak: Treats an in-memory queue as sufficient.

whyNow: A baseline makes durability, partitioning, and replication concrete responses to real gaps.

view.caption: The producer pushes messages to a single broker that the consumer pops from, with everything held in memory.

steps.title: 2. Durable Partitioned Log
steps.description: Persist each topic as an append-only log, split into partitions. Sequential appends give high write throughput; a message's offset is its position. A key hashes to a partition so a key's messages stay ordered.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why an append-only log instead of a table with a 'consumed' flag, and how do you keep throughput high?

concepts.definition: A monotonic position of a record within a partition.
concepts.whyItMatters: Consumers track progress by offset; it enables replay.

concepts.definition: Writing to the end of a file/log, not random updates.
concepts.whyItMatters: Sequential I/O is far faster than random — the basis of log throughput.

options.name: Append-only log per partition (default)
options.description: Each topic partition is a sequential append-only log on disk; a message's offset is its position, and a key hashes to a partition to keep that key's messages ordered.
pros: Sequential writes -> very high throughput.
Natural retention + replay window; offsets are cheap.
cons: No in-place update/delete; consumers must track offsets.

view.caption: The producer appends messages to an append-only log for a single partition, preserving order within it.

options.name: Row-per-message table with consumed flag
options.description: Each message is a row in a relational table with a consumed flag that the broker flips as consumers acknowledge it.
pros: Familiar; ad-hoc queries.
cons: Random updates to flip flags thrash the DB; doesn't reach log throughput.
Index bloat and vacuuming at high volume.

view.caption: The producer inserts each message as a row and the broker mutates a consumed flag in a relational message table (the rejected alternative to an append-only log).

traps.trap: Using a relational table with a consumed flag as the queue
traps.why: Constant random updates and index churn can't match sequential-append throughput.
traps.instead: Append-only log; consumers advance an offset rather than mutating rows.

traps.trap: Hashing keys to partitions but later changing the partition count
traps.why: Re-hashing scatters a key's history across partitions, breaking per-key ordering.
traps.instead: Treat partition count as semi-fixed; grow with care, or use sticky/explicit partitioning.

deepDives.title: Why the log stays fast at high throughput
points: Each partition is a series of segment files; writes only ever append to the active segment, so the OS does large sequential writes.
A sparse offset/time index per segment lets a consumer seek to an offset without scanning the whole file.
Producers batch and compress, so 1M msgs/s of ~1 KB (≈1 GB/s raw) becomes far fewer, larger I/Os.
Caught-up consumers read recent segments straight from the page cache, so a hot partition serves many groups with little extra disk I/O.
Retention/compaction delete or rewrite whole old segments rather than mutating rows in place.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Other partitions keep serving; retention reclaims space.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-partition retention/compaction; alert on disk and lag.

strong: Chooses an append-only log and explains sequential-I/O throughput.
Maps keys to partitions for ordering.
weak: Reaches for a DB table with flags.

recap.after: Durable, ordered, partitioned logs with offsets and high append throughput.
recap.newRisk: Consumers must now track offsets; partition count affects ordering.

whyNow: The log is the core data structure everything else builds on.

view.caption: The producer sends messages to a partition router that hashes each key to one of partitions 0, 1, or 2.

flows.name: Produce -> partitioned log -> consumer reads by offset
flows.note: A keyed message is routed to a partition, appended at an offset, and read sequentially by a consumer.

steps.title: 3. Consumer Groups & Offsets
steps.description: Let independent consumer groups read the same topic, each tracking its own committed offset. Within a group, partitions are balanced across members so they scale out; a coordinator reassigns partitions when members join or leave (rebalance).
steps.decisionPrompt: Three consumers in one group read a 3-partition topic. One crashes. What happens?

concepts.definition: A set of consumers that jointly consume a topic, each partition owned by one member.
concepts.whyItMatters: Gives parallel consumption with each message processed once per group.
Keeps work distributed and recovers a crashed member's partitions.

deepDives.title: Offset commit timing
points: Commit after processing for at-least-once (a crash before commit reprocesses, not loses).
Commit before processing for at-most-once (a crash loses, never duplicates).
Rebalances briefly pause consumption; keep them rare and fast (static membership, sticky assignment).

traps.trap: Committing the offset before processing the message
traps.why: A crash after commit but before processing loses the message silently.
traps.instead: Commit after successful processing (at-least-once) unless you explicitly want at-most-once.

traps.trap: Storing offsets on the consumer's local disk
traps.why: A replacement instance after a crash has no idea where to resume.
traps.instead: Commit offsets to the broker/durable store keyed by (group, partition).

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Its partitions are reassigned and resume from the last committed offset.
failureDrills.mitigation: Coordinator rebalances; uncommitted messages are redelivered to the new owner.

strong: Explains group ownership, rebalancing, and commit-after-process.
Stores offsets durably keyed by group.

recap.after: Independent groups read in parallel, resuming from durable offsets after failure.
recap.newRisk: Rebalances pause consumption; commit timing decides delivery semantics.

whyNow: Groups + offsets are how consumption scales and survives failure.

view.caption: The group coordinator assigns partitions to consumers 1 and 2 in the group, each reading its partition and committing to the consumer offsets store.

options.name: Broker-coordinated group with partition assignment (default)
options.description: A group coordinator assigns each partition to exactly one member and reassigns on join/leave, while committed offsets are stored centrally so restarts resume cleanly. Each assignment round bumps a generation number, and commits from an older generation are rejected.
pros: A group coordinator owns the partition-to-consumer assignment, so when one consumer crashes its partitions are reassigned (rebalanced) automatically.
Each partition is consumed by exactly one member, preserving per-partition ordering.
Committed offsets are stored centrally, so a restarted consumer resumes exactly where the group left off.
Generation fencing: after a rebalance the coordinator rejects commits from the old generation, so a paused or crashed consumer can't overwrite the new owner's progress when it wakes up.
cons: A rebalance pauses the whole group briefly (stop-the-world) while assignments are recomputed.
The coordinator is extra control-plane machinery to run and make highly available.
Adding/removing consumers churns assignments even for unaffected partitions.

view.caption: The broker-side coordinator assigns partitions 0 and 1 to consumers 1 and 2, which commit progress to the consumer offsets store.

options.name: Client-side cooperative assignment (no central coordinator)
options.description: Consumers agree on partition ownership among themselves via a shared store and incrementally rebalance only the partitions that actually move.
pros: Consumers agree on partition ownership among themselves (e.g. via a shared store), removing the coordinator as a moving part.
Cooperative/incremental rebalancing only reassigns the partitions that actually move, avoiding a full stop-the-world pause.
Each consumer still owns whole partitions, so ordering is preserved.
cons: Distributed agreement on ownership is subtle — split-brain can hand one partition to two consumers, breaking exactly-one ownership.
Failure detection now lives in the clients, which must heartbeat reliably.
Harder to reason about and debug than a single authoritative coordinator.

view.caption: Consumers 1 and 2 claim partitions through a shared assignment store and commit offsets without a central coordinator.

flows.name: Consumer crash -> rebalance -> resume from committed offset
flows.note: A heartbeat timeout triggers a rebalance; the new owner resumes at the last committed offset, and the dead member's stale-generation commits are fenced.

steps.title: 4. Delivery Semantics & Idempotency
steps.description: Make the guarantees explicit. The default is at-least-once (redeliver until acked), which means duplicates; achieve exactly-once effect by deduping with producer idempotency ids and idempotent consumers. True exactly-once delivery is impossible. Broker-side producer dedup gives exactly-once append to the log within a bounded producer session/window — not exactly-once processing across consume -> process -> produce, which additionally needs transactions, an outbox, or idempotent consumer-side writes.
steps.decisionPrompt: A producer's publish times out and it retries. How do you avoid a duplicate message in the log?

concepts.definition: At-least-once delivery plus dedup yields a single effect.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the achievable, useful guarantee — not exactly-once delivery.

concepts.definition: A (producer_id, producer_epoch, seq) tag the broker uses to drop duplicate appends within a bounded window.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes producer retries safe without duplicating log records; the epoch fences a zombie producer after a restart.

flows.name: Duplicate publish is deduped
flows.note: Broker drops the retry by idempotency id.

traps.why: It's impossible over an unreliable network; consumers can always crash between act and ack.
traps.instead: Provide at-least-once delivery + dedup to get exactly-once effect.

traps.trap: Non-idempotent consumers on an at-least-once queue
traps.why: Redelivery double-applies side effects (double charge, double email).
traps.instead: Make consumer processing idempotent (dedupe by message id / use upserts).

failureDrills.scenario: Consumer processes a message, then crashes before committing the offset.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The message is reprocessed, not lost; the effect happens once.

recap.after: At-least-once with idempotency gives a clear exactly-once effect.
recap.newRisk: Dedup stores and idempotent consumers are now required.

whyNow: Before relying on the queue for real work, its guarantees must be precise.

view.caption: The producer publishes with an idempotency id that the broker checks against the dedup store before appending once to the partition for the consumer.

options.name: Idempotent producer: broker dedups by producer id + sequence (default)
options.description: Each publish carries a producer id and sequence number, and the broker dedups retries within a window so a record is appended to the log at most once.
pros: A retried publish carries the same idempotency id, so the broker appends it at most once even after a timeout.
Dedup happens once at the broker, so every downstream consumer sees a single copy.
Gives exactly-once append into the log within the dedup window; end-to-end exactly-once still needs idempotent or transactional consumers.
cons: The broker must keep a dedup window (producer id -> last sequence), costing memory and bounded by a retention window.
Only dedups within that window — a very delayed duplicate beyond it can slip through.
Adds a lookup on the hot publish path.

view.caption: An idempotent producer tags messages with a producer id and sequence so the broker dedups via the dedup store and appends each only once.

options.name: At-least-once + idempotent consumers
options.description: The broker appends every publish including retries with no dedup state, and each consumer makes processing idempotent (e.g. a dedup key on write) to absorb duplicates.
pros: Broker stays dumb and fast: it just appends every publish, including retries, with no dedup state.
Duplicates are tolerated because each consumer makes processing idempotent (e.g. dedup key on write).
Simplest broker; pushes correctness to the application where the business key lives.
cons: Every consumer must implement and maintain its own dedup, duplicating effort and risking bugs.
The log itself contains duplicates, so any non-idempotent consumer is exposed.
Dedup keys and their stores add latency and storage on the consume path.

view.caption: The broker appends at-least-once (with possible duplicates) and the consumer dedups by key against its own consumer dedup store.

steps.title: 5. Queue Semantics on Top of the Log (Alternative Mode)
steps.description: This step adds a *second consumption mode*, not just another component. Offset logs and leased work queues both solve consumer failure, but they track different state: a log tracks one group *position*; a leased queue tracks per-message *ownership and retry count*. In work-queue mode a received message becomes invisible for a visibility timeout (a lease with a receipt handle); the consumer acks (deletes) on success or it reappears for retry after the timeout, and a message that fails repeatedly goes to a dead-letter queue. The final design supports both modes with separate state machines — pick log mode for ordered streaming/replay, queue mode for competing-consumer task distribution.
steps.decisionPrompt: A consumer takes a message but crashes before finishing. How does it get reprocessed without being lost or stuck forever?

concepts.definition: A window during which a delivered message is hidden from other consumers.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents two consumers processing the same message while one is still working.

concepts.definition: A side queue for messages that exceed a retry limit.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps poison messages from blocking the queue while preserving them.

traps.trap: Deleting the message as soon as it's delivered
traps.why: If the consumer then crashes, the message is gone — at-most-once by accident.
traps.instead: Hide it for a visibility timeout; delete only on ack, requeue on timeout.

traps.why: It blocks progress and burns capacity.
traps.instead: Cap retries and dead-letter it for inspection.

failureDrills.scenario: A malformed message always throws on processing.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It stops blocking the queue after a few tries.
failureDrills.mitigation: Retry limit routes it to the DLQ; the rest of the queue flows.

strong: Uses visibility timeout + ack and a DLQ with a retry cap.

deepDives.title: Log mode vs. queue mode — two different contracts
points: Log/stream mode: ack means commit the group offset; the record stays in the log until retention; a stuck record blocks ordered progress for that partition unless the app skips it or emits to an error topic. State = one offset per (group, partition).

Work-queue mode: ack means delete/complete; the broker tracks a per-message lease (receipt handle, visibility deadline, receive count); ordering is weaker (competing consumers complete out of order). State = per-message in-flight rows + a DLQ.
Don't blend them: a single consumer should pick one contract. The DLQ is native to queue mode; in log mode the equivalent is an application-level error topic.
Choose queue mode for task distribution where any worker can take any message; choose log mode for ordered replayable streams and multiple independent readers.

recap.before: A crash mid-process could lose or duplicate-block a message.
recap.after: A second, queue-style consumption mode tracks per-message leases; failures retry then dead-letter. Log mode (offsets) and queue mode (leases) now coexist as distinct contracts.
recap.newRisk: Two modes mean two state machines; visibility-timeout tuning and DLQ monitoring are now needed for queue mode.

whyNow: Once delivery semantics are set, candidates must decide whether the system is a log, a work queue, or a dual-mode broker — and not mix the two contracts in one consumer.

view.caption: The consumer reads from the partition and acks the visibility tracker, which requeues on timeout and routes a message to the dead-letter queue after max retries.

options.name: Visibility timeout + redelivery + DLQ (default)
options.description: A polled message goes invisible for a timeout; the consumer acks on success or it reappears for retry, and a message that fails repeatedly is routed to a dead-letter queue.
pros: A taken-but-unacked message becomes visible again after a timeout, so a crashed consumer's message is reprocessed, not lost.
After max retries the poison message lands in a dead-letter queue instead of blocking forever.
Works for competing consumers that process messages out of order (queue semantics).
cons: A slow consumer can exceed the timeout, causing duplicate delivery while it's still working.
Tracking per-message in-flight state and timers is extra broker bookkeeping.
Tuning the visibility timeout is a tradeoff between fast redelivery and false duplicates.

view.caption: The visibility tracker hides a delivered message until ack, requeues it to the partition on timeout, and sends it to the dead-letter queue after max retries.

options.name: Offset commit (consumer owns position)
options.description: The consumer commits the highest offset it has processed per partition, so the broker tracks one position per group rather than per-message in-flight state.
pros: No per-message in-flight tracking — the consumer just commits the highest offset it has processed.
Cheap and scalable: the broker stores one offset per partition per group, not state per message.
Reprocessing on crash is automatic — restart resumes from the last committed offset.
cons: Granularity is the partition, so one stuck message blocks the whole partition (no per-message DLQ for free).
A gap between processing and commit means redelivery of a batch, not a single message.
Doesn't fit competing-consumer fan-out where messages complete out of order.

view.caption: The consumer owns its position by committing offsets to the consumer offsets store rather than per-message acks.

flows.name: Visibility timeout & redelivery
flows.note: A polled message is hidden for the visibility timeout; an ack deletes it, otherwise it reappears (and after N failures goes to the DLQ).

else.label: no ack (crash/slow) -> redeliver, then DLQ after N tries

messages.label: after N failures, move to DLQ

steps.title: 6. Replication & Durability
steps.description: Replicate each partition from a leader to followers; the leader acks a publish only after enough followers have the record (in-sync replicas). On leader failure, a follower is promoted. This is what makes 'no loss after ack' true.
steps.decisionPrompt: When exactly do you tell the producer its message is safely stored?

concepts.definition: Followers caught up to the leader; acks wait for a quorum of them.
concepts.whyItMatters: Acking before replication risks loss if the leader then dies.

concepts.definition: Promoting a follower when the leader fails.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the partition available without losing acked data.

concepts.definition: A monotonically increasing number bumped on each leader change; a controller/metadata quorum owns per-partition leadership and the current epoch.
concepts.whyItMatters: Producers and followers carrying a stale epoch are rejected (NotLeader/fenced), so a deposed leader can't accept writes after a partition.

concepts.definition: The minimum ISR size required to accept a write.
concepts.whyItMatters: If too few replicas are in-sync, the leader refuses writes rather than risk acking data only one node holds.

deepDives.title: Control plane: who knows the leader, and how stale leaders are fenced
points: A controller / metadata quorum owns per-partition metadata: leader, replica set, ISR, leader epoch, controller epoch.
Producers and consumers fetch metadata to find the current leader; on a NotLeader/epoch-changed response they refresh metadata and retry to the new leader.
Writes require at least min-ISR in-sync replicas; otherwise the leader refuses rather than ack data only it holds.
Only in-sync replicas are eligible for election. Promoting an out-of-sync follower (unclean election) trades durability for availability and silently loses acked data.
Leader epoch fences a deposed leader: writes or replication carrying an old epoch are rejected, preventing split-brain after a network partition.

options.name: Ack after quorum/ISR replication (default)
options.description: The partition leader acks a publish only after enough in-sync follower replicas hold the record, and a follower is promoted if the leader fails.
pros: No loss of acked data if the leader fails.
Tunable durability via the required replica count.
cons: Higher publish latency; availability depends on enough replicas.

view.caption: The leader replica waits for the in-sync follower to replicate before acking, trading latency for durability.

options.name: Ack immediately on leader write
options.description: The leader acks a publish as soon as it writes the record locally, before any follower has replicated it.
pros: Lowest latency.
cons: A leader crash before replication loses acked messages — violates the durability requirement.

view.caption: The leader replica acks the producer instantly on its own write before any follower replicates.

traps.trap: Acking the producer on the leader's local write only
traps.why: If the leader dies before replicating, an 'acked' message is lost.
traps.instead: Ack after a quorum of in-sync replicas have it (tunable).

traps.trap: Electing an out-of-sync follower as leader
traps.why: Promoting a replica missing recent records silently loses acked data.
traps.instead: Only elect from in-sync replicas; refuse otherwise (or accept explicit unclean-election risk).

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: An in-sync follower takes over with no acked data lost.
failureDrills.mitigation: ISR-only leader election; producers retry to the new leader.

strong: Acks after ISR replication and elects only in-sync followers.

recap.before: A broker failure could lose acked messages.
recap.after: Acked messages survive failure via ISR replication and safe failover.
recap.newRisk: Durability costs publish latency and needs enough live replicas.

whyNow: 'No loss after ack' is a core requirement that only replication delivers.

view.caption: The controller assigns the partition leader and tracks ISR; the producer publishes to the leader, which replicates to a follower and acks only after the in-sync follower confirms.

flows.name: Ack after in-sync replication
flows.note: The leader appends, waits for enough followers to replicate, and only then acks the producer (no loss after ack).

flows.name: Leader failover with epoch fencing
flows.note: The controller detects the dead leader, elects an in-sync follower with a bumped epoch, and the producer retries to the new leader; the old leader is fenced.

messages.label: detect failure; elect in-sync follower, epoch=6

steps.title: 7. Scaling Partitions & Brokers
steps.description: Scale throughput by adding partitions and spreading partition leaders across brokers. A router maps keys to partitions; metadata tracks which broker leads each. Adding capacity means rebalancing partitions, done carefully to preserve ordering and minimize movement.
steps.decisionPrompt: One topic's throughput outgrows a single broker. What do you add, and what breaks if you do it naively?

concepts.term: Partition as the unit of parallelism
concepts.definition: More partitions = more parallel producers/consumers and brokers.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the primary scaling lever; consumer parallelism is capped by partition count.
Avoids one broker being the hot leader for everything.

options.name: More partitions, leaders spread across brokers (default)
options.description: Throughput scales by adding partitions whose leaders are spread across brokers; a router maps keys to partitions and metadata tracks which broker leads each.
pros: Scales producer and consumer throughput linearly.
Spreads load; no single hot broker.
cons: Consumer parallelism capped at partition count.
Adding partitions can disturb key->partition mapping/ordering.

view.caption: Adding more partitions lets the router spread partitions 0 and 1 (and their leaders) across more brokers.

options.name: One big partition, scale the broker
options.description: The topic stays a single partition on one broker, scaled only by making that broker bigger, preserving a total order across the whole topic.
pros: Total ordering across the topic; simple.
cons: No parallelism — one consumer at a time; single-node throughput ceiling.

view.caption: The router sends everything to a single partition and the broker itself is scaled up vertically.

deepDives.title: Repartitioning without chaos
points: Pick partition count generously up front; growing it remaps keys and disturbs per-key ordering.
Spread leaders evenly and move replicas gradually to avoid a rebalance storm.
Consumer parallelism within a group can't exceed partition count — size accordingly.

deepDives.title: Sizing from the capacity numbers
points: Target partitions ≈ total throughput ÷ per-partition throughput: ~1 GB/s at ~10 MB/s per partition ≈ 100+ partitions, with headroom.
Broker count follows from per-broker disk write throughput and NIC, including ~3× replication traffic and per-group read fan-out — not just ingress.
Too many partitions is also a cost: more metadata, more open files/memory, longer leader-election and recovery times. Don't over-shard blindly.

deepDives.title: Hot keys (skew beats average)
points: Key hashing spreads load only if keys are well-distributed; one hot key pins a whole partition and one consumer, so average throughput looks fine while one partition is saturated.
Mitigations: add a salt/sub-key to fan a hot key across partitions (giving up strict per-key order for it), use a custom partitioner, or isolate the hot tenant onto its own topic/partitions.
Detect via per-partition produce/consume lag, not just topic-level averages.

traps.trap: Requiring total ordering across the whole topic
traps.why: Total order forces a single partition, killing parallelism and throughput.
traps.instead: Scope ordering to a partition (per key); accept no cross-partition order.

traps.trap: Doubling partitions under load with no plan
traps.why: Keys remap, breaking per-key ordering and confusing stateful consumers.
traps.instead: Over-provision partitions early; repartition rarely and deliberately.

strong: Scales via partitions, spreads leaders, scopes ordering per key.
Knows consumer parallelism is bounded by partitions.

recap.before: Throughput was capped by one broker/partition.
recap.after: Partitions and spread leaders scale throughput horizontally.
recap.newRisk: Partition count bounds parallelism and ordering scope; repartitioning is disruptive.

whyNow: Partitioning is the final scaling lever once durability and semantics are solid.

view.caption: The partition router hashes keys across partitions 0, 1, and 2, whose leaders and followers are spread across brokers for throughput.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: producers publish (with producer_id/epoch/seq) to the broker, which dedups and key-routes to a partitioned append-only log; a controller/metadata quorum owns leadership and ISR, so every partition (P0/P1/P2) is leader-replicated and acked only after the in-sync set has it — each has its own leader/follower set spread across brokers. Consumers choose a mode: stream/log mode reads in parallel from durable committed offsets with generation-fenced rebalancing on failure; work-queue mode uses per-message leases (visibility timeout), acks, and a DLQ. Dedup is broker-owned, not producer-owned.

view.caption: The producer publishes with an idempotency id to the broker, which dedups against the dedup store and routes by key; the controller owns leader/ISR metadata, and each of partitions 0/1/2 is leader-replicated to a follower. The coordinator assigns partitions to consumers 1 and 2: in log mode they commit offsets; in queue mode they ack the visibility tracker, which dead-letters on retries.

tradeoff: Self-hosting a log gives full control of retention, tuning, and cost but you own brokers, rebalancing, and disk capacity planning.
Managed/SaaS removes broker ops and scales on demand, at higher per-message cost and less low-level control.
SQS/Service Bus are work-queue-mode native (leases, DLQ); Kafka/Kinesis/Event Hubs are log-mode native (offsets).
makesIrrelevant: A fully managed, autoscaling broker can remove most manual partition/broker capacity planning.

tradeoff: An external coordinator (ZooKeeper/etcd) is operationally separate to run and keep highly available.
A built-in quorum (KRaft) folds metadata into the brokers, simplifying ops at the cost of coupling.
Throttling producers protects brokers when disk, network, or page cache saturate, but pushes the backlog upstream where it must be handled.
Dropping or shedding load keeps the broker healthy but loses messages — only acceptable for best-effort traffic.
Consumer lag and retention-expiry are the signals that matter: a consumer falling behind faster than retention silently loses data.
Watch for rebalance storms and per-partition skew, not just topic-level averages.
Topic/queue-level ACLs and per-tenant quotas prevent one noisy tenant from starving others on a shared cluster.
Per-tenant clusters give the strongest isolation at the cost of more infrastructure to run.

functional.how: Key-routed append to a partitioned log.
Consumer groups with durable committed offsets.
Leader-follower replication; ack after in-sync replicas.
Each group tracks its own offsets over the same log.
Work-queue mode: a retry cap routes poison messages to a DLQ. Log mode: the app skips and emits to an error topic.
Log mode tracks a group offset; work-queue mode tracks per-message leases, retries, and a DLQ — separate state machines on the same log.

nonFunctional.how: ISR replication and safe leader election.
Key hashing keeps a key's records ordered on one partition.
At-least-once + producer/consumer idempotency for exactly-once effect.
Add partitions and spread leaders across brokers.
Rebalance + resume from committed offsets; visibility timeout requeues in-flight.

say: Restate it: durable, ordered, high-throughput messaging with clear delivery semantics.
List functional (publish, consume, groups, DLQ) and non-functional (throughput, durability, ordering, semantics).
Pin down the guarantees early — they drive everything (at-least-once + idempotency is the usual answer).
Size msgs/s, message size, retention, and number of consumer groups.
Sketch publish, poll, and commit; note the offset is the consumer's only state.
Start in-memory to expose durability, then the append-only partitioned log.
Add consumer groups + offsets, delivery semantics + idempotency, visibility/ack + DLQ, then replication.
Go deep where pushed: exactly-once effect, ISR replication, or repartitioning.
Scale by partitions and leader spread; scope ordering per key.
Summarize how each requirement is met and name the dominant tension (ordering vs parallelism, latency vs durability).

expectations: Knows a durable log beats an in-memory queue and that consumers track an offset.
Understands a queue decouples producer and consumer.
Designs partitioning for throughput and per-key ordering, with consumer groups and offsets.
Articulates at-least-once + idempotency for exactly-once effect.
Uses visibility timeouts, acks, and a DLQ.
Specifies ISR replication, ack semantics, and safe leader election for no-loss durability.
Reasons about repartitioning, ordering-vs-parallelism, and rebalance impact.

followUps: How would you implement log compaction to retain only the latest value per key?
How do you support exactly-once across consume->process->produce (transactional messaging)?
How would you handle a slow consumer that falls far behind retention and risks data loss?
How do you do geo-replication across regions and what consistency do you give?
How would you expose backpressure to producers when brokers are saturated?
How do you monitor consumer lag and rebalance storms in production?
How would you design the DLQ replay workflow — inspecting, fixing, and re-injecting dead-lettered messages safely?
How does the controller/metadata quorum stay consistent, and what happens to producers during a leader-epoch change?

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.

links.why: Readable consensus reference for leader election, log replication, membership changes, and safety.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.

links.why: Grounds monitoring and alerting decisions in user-visible reliability signals.
